title: Exam-Day One-Pager
summary: The final-glance sheet: every must-know acronym, the predictive vs agile vs hybrid differences, the highest-yield mindset rules and hot topics, and all the formulae on one screen. Read this last, right before you walk in.

sections.heading: Must-know acronyms
sections.body:
- INVEST - Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable (good user story).
- MVP - Minimum Viable Product; WIP - Work In Progress (Kanban limits it).
- WBS - Work Breakdown Structure; RBS - Resource Breakdown Structure; RTM - Requirements Traceability Matrix; SOW - Statement of Work.
- RACI - Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed (a RAM - Responsibility Assignment Matrix).
- CCB - Change Control Board; OPA - Organisational Process Assets; EEF - Enterprise Environmental Factors.
- PMO - Project Management Office; PO - Product Owner; SME - Subject Matter Expert.
- RFI / RFQ / RFP - Request for Information / Quote / Proposal.
- FFP / FPIF / FPEPA - fixed-price family; CPFF / CPIF / CPAF - cost-reimbursable family; T&M - Time & Material.
- PDCA - Plan-Do-Check-Act; ADKAR - Awareness, Desire, Knowledge, Ability, Reinforcement.
- OKR - Objective + Key Results (strategic aim); KPI - Key Performance Indicator (ongoing tracking).
- EMV - Expected Monetary Value; EV/PV/AC, CV/SV, CPI/SPI, BAC/EAC/ETC/VAC/TCPI (earned value); PERT - three-point estimate.
- BCR - Benefit-Cost Ratio; ROI; NPV - Net Present Value; IRR - Internal Rate of Return; CoQ - Cost of Quality.
sections.tip: If you blank on an item, expand the acronym first - the words usually point at the answer.

sections.heading: Predictive vs Agile vs Hybrid
sections.body:
Read the signals, then match the answer's flavour.

- Predictive (waterfall): stable, well-understood scope; plan-heavy up front. Signals - baseline, WBS, change request, CCB, critical path, earned value. Right flavour - follow formal process, submit a change request, protect the baseline.
- Agile (adaptive): evolving requirements, frequent feedback; deliver in increments. Signals - sprint, iteration, user story, backlog, Product Owner, standup, self-organising. Right flavour - empower the team, diagnose WITH the team, let the PO own priority, use retrospectives to improve.
- Hybrid: mixes both or mentions tailoring. Right flavour - fit the tool to each part; plan and govern the predictive parts, iterate the uncertain parts; break down and prioritise the KNOWN requirements rather than waiting for all of them.

Who decides / authority: predictive - the PM plans and directs; agile - the team self-organises and the PO prioritises; the PM is a servant leader removing impediments in both agile and hybrid.

rules.meaning: stable scope, plan up front - baseline, WBS, CCB, earned value; follow the formal process and protect the baseline.
evolving scope, deliver in increments - sprints, backlog, Product Owner; empower the team and improve in retros.
a mix / tailoring - govern the known parts, iterate the uncertain; break down and prioritise what is known.

sections.tip: Sprints / backlog / PO -> agile. Baselines / WBS / CCB -> predictive. 'Tailoring' or a mix -> hybrid.

sections.heading: Highest-yield mindset rules
sections.body:
- Baseline (scope/schedule/cost) needs changing -> raise a Change Request (never edit directly). Registers and logs update freely.
- Has it happened? Yes -> Issue Log. No / might happen -> Risk Register.
- New risk spotted -> log it in the Register first, then analyse, then respond. Every response gets a Risk Owner.
- Known risk fires with a planned response -> use the response plan; don't improvise.
- Agile team struggling -> diagnose WITH the team (not PM decides, not extend the timeline).
- One person struggling -> private one-on-one; whole-team issue -> survey / bring the team together. Incentives usually backfire.
- Functional manager pulls a resource -> negotiate with the functional manager first; escalate only if that fails.
- Value in doubt / long timeline -> prototype or MVP.
- New / changed stakeholder -> Register holds the who; Engagement Plan holds the how.
- Ground rule broken -> remind directly; strengthen the process only if it recurs.
- Emotions in a conflict -> acknowledge them first, never assign blame.
- Diagnose before treating; follow process; least-drastic correct action wins.

signals.title: Baseline vs registers
signals.note: Changing a baseline needs a change request; registers and logs update freely.

signals.title: Issue vs risk
signals.note: Has it happened? Yes -> Issue Log. No / might happen -> Risk Register.

signals.title: Agile team struggling
signals.note: Diagnose WITH the team - don't decide for them or just extend the timeline.

signals.title: Resource pulled
signals.note: Negotiate with the functional manager first; escalate only if that fails.

signals.title: Least-drastic action
signals.note: Diagnose before treating; follow process; the smallest correct action wins.

sections.tip: On a close call, strike options that bypass process, escalate too early, decide for an agile team, or are passive when action is needed.

sections.heading: Roles, quality order & closure
sections.body:
- Sponsor funds and champions ONE project and signs off acceptance; PMO aligns/standardises ACROSS projects and can authorise or terminate; Product Owner owns the backlog and priority; functional manager owns the people in a matrix.
- PM authority by structure: functional (little) -> weak matrix (coordinator) -> balanced -> strong matrix (moderate-high) -> projectized (full).
- Quality: Plan Quality (set standards) -> Manage Quality (processes / audits / prevention) -> Control Quality (inspect deliverables for defects) -> Validate Scope (customer formally accepts).
- Cost of Quality: Conformance = prevention + appraisal; Nonconformance = internal + external failure.
- Closure order: accept deliverables -> close contracts/procurement -> capture lessons learned -> release the team last. Don't close until finance confirms payment.
trace: Confirm the deliverables are accepted (Validate Scope / sponsor sign-off).
Release the team last - and don't close until finance confirms payment.
sections.tip: Audit = Manage Quality; inspect deliverables = Control Quality; customer sign-off = Validate Scope. Sponsor accepts, PMO aligns.

sections.heading: Conflict, leadership, change models
sections.body:
- Conflict modes (Thomas-Kilmann): Collaborate/problem-solve (win-win, PMP-preferred) > Compromise > Smooth/accommodate > Force (win-lose, emergencies) > Withdraw/avoid.
- Tuckman: Forming -> Storming -> Norming -> Performing -> Adjourning.
- Leadership: Directive (crises/new teams), Servant (agile default), Situational (match team maturity), Transactional (rewards), Transformational (vision), Laissez-faire (expert self-directed teams).
- Motivation: Maslow (hierarchy), Herzberg (hygiene prevents dissatisfaction vs motivators drive it), McGregor (X vs Y), McClelland (achievement/affiliation/power), Vroom (effort -> performance -> valued reward).
- Change models - individual level: ADKAR, Bridges (Ending -> Neutral Zone -> New Beginning), Kubler-Ross curve. Organisation level: Lewin (unfreeze-change-refreeze), Kotter (8 steps), Satir (performance dips then recovers).
sections.tip: Conflict -> collaborate. Agile -> servant leadership. People transition lags the system change - plan for the dip.

sections.heading: Procurement & schedule quick cuts
sections.body:
- Bid docs: RFI (gather info) -> RFQ (price a well-defined buy) -> RFP (complex, solution unknown - the most formal; a new vendor needs the RFP).
- Contract risk: Fixed Price - seller carries most risk; Cost-Reimbursable - buyer carries most risk; T&M - medium (staff augmentation / small scope). FPEPA for inflation/currency on long deals.
- Procurement phases: Plan (make-or-buy) -> Conduct (RFx, select, award) -> Control (monitor vendor) -> Close (verify, pay).
- Critical path = longest path, zero float, drives duration; two paths -> critical is the longer.
- Crashing = add resources to the critical path (costs money); Fast-tracking = parallelise sequential work (adds risk). Only the critical path matters.
- Leveling can extend the schedule; smoothing stays within float. Never cut planned tests to buy time.
sections.tip: Complex/unknown solution -> RFP. Monitoring a vendor = Control Procurements; paying off = Close Procurements.

sections.heading: Formulae
sections.body:
Earned value (negative / below 1 = bad):

- CV = EV - AC; SV = EV - PV.
- CPI = EV / AC; SPI = EV / PV.
- EAC = BAC / CPI (current rate continues); ETC = EAC - AC; VAC = BAC - EAC.
- TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC) (efficiency still needed to hit BAC).

Estimating:

- PERT (three-point): tE = (O + 4M + P) / 6; Std Dev = (P - O) / 6.

Risk & selection:

- EMV = Probability x Impact (sum identified risks to size contingency).
- BCR = Benefits / Costs (above 1 = worthwhile; below 1 = costs win, consult the sponsor). NPV higher = better; IRR higher = better; shorter payback = better.

Schedule & comms:

- Total Float = LS - ES = LF - EF (critical-path activities have zero total float).
- Communication channels = n(n - 1) / 2, where n = number of people.

Procurement:

- Point of Total Assumption (FPIF) = ((Ceiling Price - Target Price) / buyer's share ratio) + Target Cost.

rules.meaning: estimate at completion if the current cost efficiency continues.

rules.term: CPI = EV / AC  ·  SPI = EV / PV
rules.meaning: cost / schedule efficiency; below 1 = over budget / behind.
expected monetary value; sum identified risks to size contingency.
three-point estimate, weighting the most-likely by 4.

sections.tip: Variance = EV minus (PV or AC); index = EV over (PV or AC). EAC = BAC / CPI. Channels = n(n-1)/2. PERT weights the middle by 4.

source: Compact synthesis of all PM Cram cheat-sheet topics for a final pre-exam review
